text: Laparoscopic Chimera Duodenum Duodenostomy for Duodenal Atresia. Our patient is a five-day-old male newborn with a past medical history of birth at thirty-seven weeks gestation via c-section of a thirty-six-year-old female with a pregnancy without complications.

the weight at birth is two point nine kilograms and length is forty five centimeters postnatally our patient was diagnosed with down syndrome and a systolic murmur chief complaint was intolerance to oral feeding since birth and bilious vomiting patient was treated in a primary care hospital with orogastic discompression electrolyte and fluid reposition and referred to our tertiary care pediatric hospital

Preoperative studies were obtained with a plain abdominal x-ray which showed important dilatation of gastric chamber and a double bubble sign. Upper GI series with hydrosoluble contrast demonstrated an obstruction at the duodenum. Bulbulus with intestinal malrotation was excluded with a colon enema.

Diagnosis of duodenum atresia or stenosis was made and surgery proposed. Pediatric cardiology department diagnosed pattern ductus arteriosus and a ventricular sept defect with no hemodynamic repercussion and no surgical contraindication. Informed consent was obtained and surgery undertaken.

The patient is positioned supine in a frog-legged position with strappings on the legs and thorax. A 5mm trocar is inserted at the infrabilical fold using an open technique, followed by the placement of a 30-degree telescope and two additional laparoscopic ports. The second trocar is positioned below the ribcage at the midclavicular line, and the third is placed at the right midclavicular line above the umbilicus. Pneumoperitoneum pressure is maintained between 5 to 8 mmHg.

During laparoscopy, the liver is retracted using a percutaneous stay suture in the falciform ligament, with an additional suture placed at the right hepatic lobe for better exposure. The surgeon visually confirms the exclusion of intestinal malrotation under laparoscopic vision.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and mobilizes the right colon at the hepatic flexure, exposing the dilated upper duodenum and the inferior duodenum. A gentle dissection is performed using a laparoscopic approach to free the jejunum.

The surgeon suspends the upper end of the duodenum using a percutaneous suture, facilitating the dissection of the distal duodenum, which appears to have a smaller diameter. Care is taken to ensure no other intestinal structures mimic the distal end of the duodenum.

The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the upper duodenum using laparoscopic scissors, followed by advancing the orogastric tube under laparoscopic guidance to ensure no duodenal webs are present. Subsequently, the inferior duodenum is incised longitudinally to maintain the anastomosis's caliber and patency.

The surgeon performs an Akimura diamond anastomosis using a polydioxanone suture, starting at the left corner of the transverse incision and extending to the midpoint of the longitudinal incision in the distal duodenum. The suture is placed percutaneously, with both ends exteriorized, and the process is repeated on the right side. The suspension of the upper duodenum is then loosened to achieve a more physiological and less tense anastomosis.

The surgeon creates the posterior side of the anastomosis using extracorporeal laparoscopic interrupted knots, beginning at the midline. The anterior side of the anastomosis is completed using the same technique, while the lateral knots are tied intracorporeally.

The surgeon tests the patency and impermeability of the anastomosis by injecting twenty milliliters of saline solution and air through the orogastric tube. A closed drain is placed and exteriorized through a five millimeter port, while the orogastric tube remains in place to decompress the stomach and monitor motility.
Results were a type three duodenal atresia, Operative time was a hundred and eighty minutes and an estimated blood loss of ten milliliters.

In the postoperative period there was a complication due to pneumonia. A day postoperative six upper GI series was made to prove patency of anastomosis and absence of leaks. Enteral feedings through orogastric tube was started and patient was discharged after antibiotic treatment.
